revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Don Philippi]][[ja:ドナルド・L・フィリッパイ]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1930|10|2}}|death_date={{death date|1993|1|26}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|United States|qid=Q30|1=United States}}|occupation={{ill|translator|qid=Q333634|1=Translator}}|awards={{ill|Fulbright Scholarship|qid=Q253936|1=Fulbright Program}}|given_name={{ill|Don|qid=Q16275555|1=Don (given name)}}}}
'''Donald L. Philippi''' (October 2, 1930 – January 26, 1993) was a noted translator of {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} and {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu language}}, and a musician. Born in Los Angeles, Philippi studied at the {{ill|University of Southern California|en|University of Southern California|qid=Q4614|lt=University of Southern California|1=University of Southern California}} before going to Japan in 1957 on a [[:en:Fulbright scholarship|Fulbright scholarship]] to study at the {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|qid=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}}. In Japan he became an expert in both {{ill|Classical Japanese|en|Classical Japanese|qid=Q1332057|lt=Classical Japanese|1=Classical Japanese}} and Ainu. Philippi is known for his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ancient Shinto prayers known as ''[[norito]]''. He also published a book of translations of Ainu epic poems (''{{ill|Yukar|en|Yukar|qid=Q1193421|lt=Yukar|1=Yukar}}''), ''Songs of Gods, Songs of Humans: The Epic Tradition of the Ainu'', and a book of translations of ancient Japanese poems, ''This Wine of Peace, This Wine of Laughter: A Complete Anthology of Japan's Earliest Songs''. Philippi was also a noted technical translator. Under the pseudonym Slava Ranko, Philippi edited and published ''Maratto'', a {{ill|little magazine|en|little magazine|qid=Q111973269|lt=little magazine|1=Little magazine}} focused on Ainu literature and culture. The first issue was published March 3, 1977 in San Francisco. Philippi was also a musician, learning the {{ill|Shamisen|en|Shamisen|qid=Q241337|lt=Shamisen|1=Shamisen}} and the {{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|qid=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}} both in the U.S. and Japan. In the late 1970s, he became familiar in the San Francisco music scene, again using the name Slava Ranko, and performing a combination of biwa and synthesizer music. In 1981, he issued an album, ''Arctic Hysteria''. ==Publications== * Philippi, Donald L. 1968–1969. ''Kojiki''. Princeton, N.J.: Princeton University Press and Tokyo: University of Tokyo Press. (ISBN 978-0691061603) == External links == * [http://www.jai2.com/dlpobit.htm Don Philippi obituary] * [http://www.f.waseda.jp/buda/tjt/tjt-idx.html Don Philippi's Technical Japanese Translation] {{Authority control|qq=}} <
{{US-translator-stub|qq=}}
}}
